Adversarially review a generated HLD session. Be a skeptic — find REAL defects, review-only, DO NOT modify files.

Workspace: /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/969a289a-8e87-431a-b5bd-fbefd2aedb60_hld/_response.md (+ maybe capacity.py). Problem: Design a Ride-Sharing Service (Uber/Lyft). Emphasis: driver-rider matching with geo-index (S2/geohash), the location-update write firehose from moving drivers, matching as a per-cell operation + hot dense cities, trip state machine, surge as a deep dive.

Report PASS/FAIL with evidence on each:
1. ALL 11 sections present and fully expanded; md > 20,000 chars. §1 must OPEN with the 🔒 scope-lock (forking assumptions FIRST), then derived functional requirements, then provisional NFRs. §2 must be the residual forks and must NOT re-ask anything §1 already locked (flag any duplication).
2. CAPACITY (§3): numbers actually derived + self-consistent (re-run capacity.py with /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python if present); a named decision-forcing number + flip threshold; a peak-from-average burst factor; no false precision.
3. TECHNICAL CORRECTNESS for THIS problem + the emphasis: are the deep dives the genuinely hardest parts (binding bottleneck, hot-key, global coordinator, consistency/read-your-own-writes, back-pressure)? Find any wrong or hand-wavy claim.
4. MERMAID SAFETY (CRITICAL — many diagrams): scan EVERY ```mermaid block; FAIL any label/edge-text containing breaking chars — parentheses, a colon, a semicolon, slashes, quotes, angle brackets, curly braces. Report the exact offending line. Confirm flowchart TD for multi-path.
5. §5 has real REST endpoints + JSON + idempotency + object-level authz + versioning/pagination noted. §7 has 5-part storage cards + PART D data lifecycle. §9 covers back-pressure, observability, rate-limiting. §10 has CAP/PACELC line.
6. Markdown hygiene: no LaTeX, no stray tags, tables well-formed, emoji-script structure present.

Return verdict SHIP or FIX-FIRST + findings (severity/where/problem/fix). Mermaid breaking-char issues are BLOCKER. Only BLOCKER/MAJOR block.

content.content:
1	# Design a Ride-Sharing Service (Uber/Lyft)
2	
3	> A rider asks for a ride from A to B, we match a nearby driver, both watch the trip move on a live map, and at the end we compute a fare and charge a card. The whole thing turns on one hard fact: **a million cars are moving and pinging their GPS every few seconds**, and we have to find the right one for you in under two seconds.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## 1. Scope, Requirements & Assumptions
8	
9	> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock scope with a couple of quick assumptions, state the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one flow at a time — request, match, track, fare — then go deep on the two or three hardest parts: the location-update firehose, per-cell matching in dense cities, and surge pricing. I'll check in with you as I go. Sound good?"
10	
11	The core tension in one line: this is a **real-time geospatial matching problem riding on top of a money-correct trip state machine** — the hard part isn't storing trips, it's that a million moving drivers generate a write firehose, and we have to query "who's near me right now" against constantly-moving data, fastest in exactly the cities that are most crowded.
12	
13	### 🔒 Scope lock (assumptions I'm committing to first — veto any of these)
14	
15	These four answers FORK the architecture. I'll state each as a decision I'm making, not an open question — stop me if your context differs:
16	
17	1. **Global, multi-region, but a trip is region-local.** I'll assume we operate worldwide but a single ride (rider, driver, matching) lives entirely within one geographic region — nobody is matched across an ocean. This lets me geo-shard everything and avoid cross-region writes on the hot path. *(If trips were genuinely cross-region this collapses — but they're not.)*
18	2. **Real-time matching, not scheduled/pooled rides.** I'll assume the core is on-demand 1:1 matching (one rider, one driver, now). Pooling (UberPool) and pre-scheduled rides are **out of scope** for the core design — they're a matching-policy layer on top.
19	3. **We integrate a payment processor, we don't BE one.** I'll assume Stripe/Braintree-style processor; we hold tokens, never raw card numbers (PCI out of scope). Fare correctness and idempotent charging are in scope; the card rails are not.
20	4. **Reads of "where's my driver" dominate writes of trip state, but the location firehose dwarfs both.** The decision-driving volume is driver GPS pings, not trip CRUD.
21	
22	> 🤝 **Scope checkpoint:** "So I'm treating in-scope as: estimate, request, match, live-track, and fare+pay — single-region per trip, real-time 1:1, external payments. Out of scope: pooling, scheduled rides, driver onboarding/KYC, ratings, the maps/routing engine itself (I'll treat ETA as a service call). Add or cut anything before I commit?"
23	
24	### Functional requirements (the core flows — "above the line")
25	
26	These are derived directly from the locked assumptions. Each is exactly one diagrammed slice in §6.
27	
28	1. **Rider gets a fare estimate for A→B before committing.**
29	   - 🗣️ *Plain words:* before you tap "request," you see "about $14, 4 min away" so you can decide.
30	   - ⚡ *Why it's hard:* the estimate depends on live supply/demand (surge), so it's not a static price table — it's a real-time read of a moving market.
31	2. **Rider requests a ride and we match a nearby available driver.**
32	   - 🗣️ *Plain words:* you tap "request" and within a couple seconds a specific driver is on the way.
33	   - ⚡ *Why it's hard:* "nearby" is a geospatial query against data that's moving every 4 seconds, and we must not offer the same driver to two riders.
34	3. **Driver accepts (or declines) the offer; we lock the pairing.**
35	   - 🗣️ *Plain words:* the driver's phone buzzes with your trip; they tap accept and now you're paired.
36	   - ⚡ *Why it's hard:* an offer is a soft lock with a timeout — if the driver doesn't answer in ~15s we must release them and re-match without double-booking.
37	4. **Both track the trip live on a map until drop-off.**
38	   - 🗣️ *Plain words:* you watch the little car move toward you, then watch your own dot move to the destination.
39	   - ⚡ *Why it's hard:* this is a sustained low-latency stream of the *firehose* filtered down to the two people who care, surviving tunnels and dropped signal.
40	5. **At drop-off we compute the fare and charge the rider, paying out the driver.**
41	   - 🗣️ *Plain words:* the trip ends, you get charged the right amount once, the driver gets paid.
42	   - ⚡ *Why it's hard:* money must be exactly-once — a retry or a crash mid-charge cannot double-bill.
43	
44	**Below the line (out of scope for this pass):** UberPool/shared rides, scheduled rides, driver onboarding & background checks, ratings & reviews, in-app chat, the routing/maps engine internals, fraud/ML risk scoring, promotions engine.
45	
46	### Non-Functional requirements (provisional targets, tagged to assumptions)
47	
48	1. **Matching latency: p99 under ~2s to a driver offer** (assuming single-region per trip).
49	   - 🗣️ *Plain words:* 99 out of 100 ride requests get a driver offered within two seconds.
50	   - 💥 *What breaks without it:* riders stare at a spinner and cancel; drivers go idle — both sides leave the platform.
51	2. **Location-track freshness: driver dot under ~5s stale** (assuming 4s ping interval).
52	   - 🗣️ *Plain words:* the car on your map is never more than a few seconds behind where it really is.
53	   - 💥 *What breaks without it:* the car "teleports," riders think the app is broken, drivers get blamed for being late.
54	3. **Trip & payment correctness: strongly consistent, exactly-once charging** (assuming external processor).
55	   - 🗣️ *Plain words:* you're billed the right amount exactly one time, even if the network hiccups.
56	   - 💥 *What breaks without it:* double charges, chargebacks, lawsuits, trust gone.
57	4. **Availability: matching path 99.9% ("three nines"), payment path 99.99% ("four nines").**
58	   - 🗣️ *Plain words:* matching can be down ~8.7 hours/year worst case; the money path under ~52 min/year.
59	   - 💥 *What breaks without it:* an outage during Friday rush strands a whole city.
60	5. **Location-write durability is BEST-EFFORT, trip/payment durability is RPO≈0.**
61	   - 🗣️ *Plain words:* losing one GPS ping is fine (another comes in 4s); losing a payment record is never fine.
62	   - 💥 *What breaks without it:* if we tried to durably replicate every ping synchronously we'd never keep up with a million writes/s.
63	
64	> 💬 "The thing I want to flag up front: the firehose of driver locations and the correctness of money are two completely different problems wearing one app — I'll design them with different storage and different guarantees."
65	
66	> 🎙️ **Script:** "At its heart this is five flows: estimate, request, match, track, and pay. The first four are real-time and geospatial — find a nearby car, watch it move — and I'll happily trade a little staleness there for speed. The fifth, money, I won't compromise: it's strongly consistent and charged exactly once. The non-functionals split the same way — sub-two-second matching and a near-live map on one side, and exactly-once billing on the other. The reason this problem is interesting isn't the trip record; it's that a million cars are pinging GPS every few seconds and we have to answer 'who's near me' against that moving target fastest in the densest cities."
67	
68	---
69	
70	## 2. The Forks Behind My Assumptions (what changes if I'm wrong)
71	
72	The three locked assumptions where my design is most sensitive. I'm not re-asking these — they're already committed in §1 — but here's where I'd want a redirect.
73	
74	**Fork 1 — I assumed a trip is region-local (no cross-region matching).**
75	- ↔️ *What changes if I'm wrong:*
76	  - If trips really are region-local (my bet) → I geo-shard the location index and the trip store by region/cell, and the hot path never crosses a region boundary. Simple, fast.
77	  - If you actually need cross-region matching (e.g. airport pickups across a metro that spans two shards, or border cities) → I need a cell-boundary stitching layer that queries neighboring shards, and a global routing decision on which region "owns" the trip. That's a real complication I'd scope as a follow-up.
78	- 🗣️ *How I'd say it:* "I'm assuming a ride lives in one region — if you've got metros that straddle shard boundaries, tell me and I'll add boundary stitching."
79	
80	**Fork 2 — I assumed real-time 1:1 matching, no pooling.**
81	- ↔️ *What changes if I'm wrong:*
82	  - If 1:1 only (my bet) → matching is "find the best single nearby driver and offer." A greedy per-request operation.
83	  - If pooling is core → matching becomes a *batched* optimization (group riders whose routes overlap, assign one driver to many), which means a windowed batch matcher, not a per-request greedy one — a fundamentally different matching engine.
84	- 🗣️ *How I'd say it:* "I'm doing one rider one driver — if pooling's in scope, the matcher becomes a batch route-overlap optimizer and I'd design that separately."
85	
86	**Fork 3 — I assumed location writes are best-effort, not durable.**
87	- ↔️ *What changes if I'm wrong:*
88	  - If best-effort (my bet) → the live index is in-memory Redis, pings overwrite, and a lost ping just waits 4s for the next. This is what makes 1M writes/s affordable.
89	  - If every breadcrumb must be durable on the hot path → I'd have to synchronously persist a million writes/s, which forces a log-structured ingest and roughly 5-10x the write-tier cost. I'd push back hard on needing this.
90	- 🗣️ *How I'd say it:* "I'm treating GPS pings as disposable on the hot path — if regulators need every breadcrumb durably, that's a much bigger write tier and I'd want to discuss it."
91	
92	> 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "Those are the three places my design bends. Happy with region-local, real-time 1:1, best-effort pings? If so I'll size it and start drawing."
93	
94	---
95	
96	## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)
97	
98	I computed these with a small script (`capacity.py`) rather than hand-waving. **QPS** = queries per second; **ping** = one GPS location update from a driver's phone; **geo cell** = a small square of the map (think a few city blocks) we use to bucket drivers.
99	
100	| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
101	|---|---|---|---|---|
102	| **Location writes (PEAK)** | 1.5M drivers online avg × 3 burst = 4.5M online; ÷ 4s ping interval = 1,125,000/s | **~1.1M/s** | "about a million location writes a second" | **THE bottleneck.** Forces an in-memory, geo-sharded index — no disk DB survives this. |
103	| **Geo shards needed** | 1.1M/s ÷ ~100K/s per node ceiling = ~11 | **~11+ shards** | "ten-plus shards just for locations" | Geo-shard the index by cell prefix. **Flip:** any single node >100K/s ⇒ split. |
104	| **Match QPS (PEAK)** | 100M DAU × 0.3 trips/day = 30M/day; ÷ 86,400 = 347/s; × 3 burst = ~1,042/s | **~1K/s** | "around a thousand match requests a second" | Modest — matching is cheap *per request*; the cost is the read-scan in dense cells. |
105	| **Hot-cell read scan** | 2,000 drivers in one downtown cell × 9 cells (self + 8 neighbors) = 18,000 candidate rows scanned per match | **~18K rows/match** | "eighteen thousand candidates for one match downtown" | Forces ranked top-K, not a full scan; the dense-city cell is the hot key. |
106	| **Hot-cell write QPS** | 2,000 drivers ÷ 4s = 500/s onto ONE shard | **~500/s on one shard** | "one downtown cell hammers a single shard" | The hot cell is BOTH a read-scan AND write hotspot ⇒ sub-shard the cell. |
107	| **Live geo-index RAM** | 4.5M peak online × 120 B/entry = ~540 MB | **~540 MB** | "half a gig of live positions" | Tiny — the index is a RAM + throughput problem, not a storage one. |
108	| **Location history (biggest table)** | 375K/s avg × 86,400 × 30 days × 40 B × 1.5 = ~58 TB/mo | **~58 TB/mo** | "about sixty terabytes a month of breadcrumbs" | Largest table by far; needs cold-tier + partition-drop retention. |
109	| **Trip store** | 30M trips/day × 1 KB × 3 replicas × 30 = ~2.7 TB/mo | **~2.7 TB/mo** | "a couple terabytes of trips a month" | Small — a sharded SQL/NoSQL store is plenty. |
110	| **$/1M location updates** | $200K/mo write tier ÷ 972B updates/mo = $0.206 | **~$0.21 / 1M** | "about twenty cents per million pings" | Unit economic — the firehose is the dominant infra cost, not the trip DB. |
111	
112	**The ONE decision-forcing number:** ~1.1M location writes/s at peak. A single in-memory node tops out around ~100K writes/s before tail latency blows up, so we cross the line at the very first node — we **must** geo-shard the location index, and we must NOT route those writes through a durable disk database.
113	
114	**Match latency budget (sums to SLO):** client↔gateway TLS ~80ms + auth/route ~20ms + geo read ~30ms + rank/ETA ~50ms + push offer to driver ~400ms = **~580ms system time**, comfortably inside the 2s SLO. The remaining ~1.4s is the human driver tapping "accept" — which is why the *system* target is the offer, not the accept.
115	
116	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The number that drives everything is about a million location writes a second at peak. One node handles maybe a hundred thousand, so I'm sharding the location index from the start, by geography. Match requests are only about a thousand a second — cheap — so I'll spend my budget on absorbing that write firehose and on the dense-city cells where one downtown block can pin a single shard. Storage's a non-issue for trips, a couple terabytes a month; the only big table is the raw GPS breadcrumb history at around sixty terabytes a month, and that gets a retention window."
117	
118	---
119	
120	## 4. Core Entities
121	
122	A first-draft scaffold — full fields land in §7.
123	
124	- **Rider** — a passenger account; owns the request, the trip-from-their-side, and the payment method.
125	  - 🗣️ *Analogy:* the person standing on the curb with the app open.
126	  - ⚠️ *Probe:* "What if a rider requests two rides at once?" → we enforce one active trip per rider via a unique active-trip constraint.
127	- **Driver** — a person+vehicle that goes online, emits location, and accepts trips.
128	  - 🗣️ *Analogy:* the car cruising around with the driver app open, broadcasting "I'm here, I'm free."
129	  - ⚠️ *Probe:* "How do you know a driver is available?" → a status field in the live geo-index (online/offered/on-trip), not in a slow DB.
130	- **DriverLocation** — the live, ephemeral position+status of an online driver; the thing in the firehose.
131	  - 🗣️ *Analogy:* the constantly-updating GPS dot, like the blinking blue dot on your own phone but for every driver.
132	  - ⚠️ *Probe:* "Where does this live?" → in-memory geo-index (Redis), keyed by geo cell, overwritten every ping — never the durable store of record.
133	- **Trip** — the durable record of one ride and its lifecycle; the money-correct entity.
134	  - 🗣️ *Analogy:* the receipt-in-progress — it knows who, the route, the fare, and exactly what stage it's at.
135	  - ⚠️ *Probe:* "What stops a trip getting stuck?" → it's a state machine with timeouts on every transition (offer expires, no-show, etc.).
136	- **MatchOffer** — a short-lived soft-lock: "driver X has 15s to accept rider Y."
137	  - 🗣️ *Analogy:* a restaurant holding your table for 10 minutes — reserved, but released if you don't show.
138	  - ⚠️ *Probe:* "Can two riders get offered the same driver?" → no — creating an offer atomically flips the driver to `offered`; a second match skips them.
139	- **Payment** — the charge+payout tied to a completed trip.
140	  - 🗣️ *Analogy:* the credit-card slip you sign at the end.
141	  - ⚠️ *Probe:* "Exactly-once?" → keyed by trip_id as the idempotency key, so a retry returns the same charge, never a second one.
142	
143	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The entities are Rider, Driver, the live DriverLocation, the durable Trip, a short-lived MatchOffer, and the Payment. The one that surprises people is MatchOffer — it's not just 'driver accepted,' it's a soft lock with a timeout that prevents double-booking a driver. And DriverLocation deliberately does NOT live in the trip database — it's ephemeral, in memory. I'll flesh out the full fields, keys, and stores in the data model."
144	
145	---
146	
147	## 5. API / Interface
148	
149	REST over HTTPS, all paths prefixed `/v1` (additive fields don't bump the version; breaking changes go to `/v2`). The caller's identity always comes from the **JWT** *(a signed token proving who you are — we read the rider/driver id from it, never trust an id in the body)*. List endpoints use **cursor pagination** *(an opaque "where I left off" token, not offset/limit — offset gets slow and skips rows as data shifts under it)*.
150	
151	**A. Estimate a fare — `POST /v1/estimates`** (POST, not GET: it's a server-side computation over live market state, and we want it in the body, not a cacheable URL).
152	- `pickup: {lat,lng}` / `dropoff: {lat,lng}` — the route; server computes distance+ETA, **never trusts a client-sent price**.
153	- `productType: string` — UberX vs XL etc.
154	```
155	// Request
156	POST /v1/estimates
157	{ "pickup": {"lat": 40.7580, "lng": -73.9855}, "dropoff": {"lat": 40.7128, "lng": -74.0060}, "productType": "uberx" }
158	
159	// Response 200
160	{ "estimateId": "est_9f2", "fareLow": 1380, "fareHigh": 1620, "currency": "usd", "surgeMultiplier": 1.4, "etaSeconds": 240 }
161	```
162	- 🔑 *Key decision:* the response is a **non-binding quote with an `estimateId`** the request reuses, so the price the rider saw is the price we honor even if surge moves a second later.
163	
164	**B. Request a ride — `POST /v1/trips`** (POST creates new server state — a Trip).
165	- `estimateId: string` — pins the quoted price; server re-validates it's fresh.
166	- `pickup/dropoff` — re-sent for integrity; server compares to the estimate.
167	- `Idempotency-Key: <uuid>` (header) — a double-tap returns the **same** trip, never two.
168	```
169	// Request
170	POST /v1/trips
171	Idempotency-Key: c1d2e3f4
172	{ "estimateId": "est_9f2", "pickup": {"lat":40.7580,"lng":-73.9855}, "dropoff": {"lat":40.7128,"lng":-74.0060} }
173	
174	// Response 202
175	{ "tripId": "trip_7ab", "status": "MATCHING", "estimatedFare": 1500 }
176	```
177	- 🔑 *Key decision:* returns **202 with `status: MATCHING`**, not a synchronous driver — matching is async (a few seconds), so we acknowledge fast and stream the match via the track channel. `status` is server-set; the client can't forge `MATCHED`.
178	
179	**C. Driver emits location — `POST /v1/drivers/location`** (high-frequency; the firehose).
180	- `lat,lng,heading,ts` — server stamps its own receive-time too (trust-but-verify the client clock).
181	- 🔑 *Key decision:* this is **fire-and-forget, best-effort, not in a transaction** — a dropped ping is fine. In practice many clients use a persistent WebSocket/gRPC stream instead of one HTTP call per ping to cut overhead. No idempotency key needed — last-write-wins by timestamp.
182	
183	**D. Driver accepts/declines an offer — `POST /v1/offers/{offerId}/accept`** (POST: it transitions state).
184	- Object-level **authz:** we verify the JWT's driver id matches the offer's target driver — driver A cannot accept driver B's offer.
185	- 🔑 *Key decision:* the accept is the **atomic compare-and-set** — only valid if the offer is still `PENDING` and unexpired; a late accept returns `409 Conflict`.
186	
187	**E. Track a trip — `GET /v1/trips/{tripId}/stream`** (a long-lived stream — WebSocket/SSE — not polling).
188	- Object-level authz: only the trip's rider or driver may subscribe.
189	```
190	// Server pushes, every few seconds:
191	{ "tripId":"trip_7ab", "driverLoc":{"lat":40.7551,"lng":-73.9840}, "etaSeconds":180, "status":"EN_ROUTE" }
192	```
193	- 🔑 *Key decision:* pushes deltas over a persistent connection so the map updates without the client hammering us; reconnect resumes from the last known position.
194	
195	**F. Complete + pay — handled server-side on `driver→COMPLETED` transition**, exposed as `GET /v1/trips/{tripId}` for the receipt. Charging is internal and idempotent on `tripId`.
196	
197	> 💬 "Two things I'd say out loud: the price comes from the server's estimate, never the request body, so nobody hacks a cheap ride; and requesting a ride returns 202-MATCHING — I acknowledge instantly and stream the actual driver as it happens."
198	
199	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The rider flow is: POST an estimate to get a quote with an id, POST a trip referencing that id with an idempotency key so a double-tap is one ride, then open a track stream. The driver flow is a firehose of best-effort location POSTs plus an atomic accept on an offer. Everything's versioned under /v1, lists use cursor tokens, and I authorize at the object level — you can only accept your own offer and watch your own trip."
200

202	
203	## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time)
204	
205	Shared palette across all diagrams: blue = client apps, green = our services, amber = queues/streams, grey = datastores, purple = external.
206	
207	### 6.1 "Rider gets a fare estimate for A→B"
208	
209	The rider needs a price and ETA before committing. This slice introduces the read path into the live market and the routing/ETA dependency.
210	
211	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
212	|---|---|---|---|---|
213	| **API Gateway** | The front door that terminates TLS, checks the JWT, and routes requests | One place for auth, rate-limiting, and routing so services stay simple | Not per-service auth (duplicated logic, easy to get wrong) | One more hop (~20ms) on every request |
214	| **Estimate Service** | A stateless service that computes distance, ETA, and applies surge | Pricing logic in one place, scales horizontally | Not in the client (client can't be trusted with price) | Needs live surge data, so it reads the surge store |
215	| **Routing/ETA Service** | External maps engine that turns two points into distance+time | Building our own routing is years of work; treat ETA as a call | Not Haversine straight-line (ignores traffic, roads — wrong) | External dependency; we cache and degrade if it's slow |
216	| **Surge Store (Redis)** | In-memory map of geo-cell → current surge multiplier | Surge changes by the minute; needs a fast read keyed by cell | Not a SQL table (read-per-estimate at 1K/s is wasteful) | Surge is eventually consistent (seconds stale — fine) |
217	
218	> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
219	> **API Gateway** — the single entry point that authenticates and routes every request. Here it lets every backend service trust that the caller is already identified.
220	> **Surge multiplier** — a number like 1.4 that multiplies the base fare when demand outstrips supply in an area. Here it's read live per estimate so the quote reflects the current market.
221	> **ETA** — estimated time of arrival, computed by a routing engine over the road network and traffic. Here it both prices the ride and tells the rider how long until pickup.
222	
223	**Diagram — fare estimate read path:**
224	```mermaid
225	flowchart TD
226	    R[Rider app]:::client
227	    R -->|"1 POST estimate"| GW[API Gateway]:::svc
228	    GW -->|"2 forward"| EST[Estimate Service]:::svc
229	    EST -->|"3 distance and ETA"| RT[Routing ETA Service]:::ext
230	    EST -->|"4 read surge for cell"| SG[(Surge Store Redis)]:::store
231	    EST -->|"5 quote with estimateId"| GW
232	    GW -->|"6 200 quote"| R
233	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
234	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
235	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
236	    classDef ext fill:#241a2e,stroke:#a78bfa,stroke-width:2px,color:#ede9fe
237	```
238	
239	**Numbered narration:**
240	1. Rider POSTs pickup+dropoff. *Why gateway:* it owns auth so the Estimate Service never re-checks the token. *State:* none yet — read-only. *What if it fails:* gateway returns 401/429; rider retries.
241	2. Gateway forwards to Estimate Service. *Why:* pricing is centralized server-side. *State:* none. *What if:* gateway load-balances to a healthy replica.
242	3. Estimate Service asks Routing for distance+time. *Why:* roads and traffic, not straight lines. *State:* none. *What if Routing is slow:* fall back to a cached/region-average ETA and flag the quote as approximate.
243	4. It reads the current surge for the pickup cell. *Why Redis:* surge is hot and changes by the minute. *State:* none. *What if surge store is down:* default to 1.0x (no surge) — fail open on price.
244	5. It returns a quote with an `estimateId`. *State:* the quote is briefly cached so the request can pin it. *What if:* if nothing pins it, it just expires.
245	6. Rider sees "~$15, 4 min."
246	
247	> 💬 "The key insight here: the price is computed server-side from live surge, and we hand back an id so the price the rider saw is the price we honor."
248	
249	> 🎙️ **Script:** "For an estimate, the rider posts the two points, the gateway authenticates, and the Estimate Service combines a real routing call for distance and time with a live surge multiplier read from Redis. I deliberately don't price in the client — that'd be trivially hackable — and I treat routing as an external service with a cached fallback so a slow maps call degrades to an approximate quote instead of a spinner. We hand back an estimate id to pin the price."
250	
251	### 6.2 "Rider requests a ride and we match a nearby driver"
252	
253	This is the heart. The rider commits; we find a nearby available driver from the live geo-index. New components: the Trip Service (state machine), the Matching Service, and the live Geo-Index.
254	
255	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
256	|---|---|---|---|---|
257	| **Trip Service** | Owns the Trip state machine and the durable trip record | Money/state correctness needs one authoritative owner with transactions | Not letting matching write trips directly (no single owner ⇒ races) | A hop between match and trip persistence |
258	| **Matching Service** | Stateless worker that reads candidate drivers for a cell and ranks them | Matching is a read-scan + score; horizontally scalable per region | Not matching inside the geo-index (mixing storage with logic) | Must read the index, so it's co-located by region |
259	| **Geo-Index (Redis, sharded by cell)** | In-memory store of every online driver's position, keyed by geo cell | ~1.1M writes/s + sub-30ms "who's near me" — only RAM survives this | Not Postgres/PostGIS (can't take a million writes/s); not S3 (not real-time) | Best-effort, ephemeral — a lost ping waits 4s |
260	| **Geocell library (S2/geohash)** | A scheme that maps a lat/lng to a small grid cell id | Lets "near me" become "same cell + 8 neighbors" — a key lookup, not a distance scan | Not raw lat/lng range scans (slow, no clean sharding key) | Cell edges mean we scan neighbors too |
261	
262	> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
263	> **Geo-Index** — an in-memory map from geo cell to the drivers currently in it. Here it answers "which drivers are near this pickup" in milliseconds without scanning a database.
264	> **S2 / geohash** — schemes that encode a latitude/longitude into a short string/integer for a grid cell. Here they turn a proximity search into a fast key lookup on a cell and its neighbors.
265	> **State machine** — a model where an entity can only move between defined states (MATCHING → MATCHED → EN_ROUTE → COMPLETED). Here it guarantees a trip can't skip or repeat a stage.
266	
267	**Diagram — match flow:**
268	```mermaid
269	flowchart TD
270	    R[Rider app]:::client
271	    R -->|"1 POST trip"| GW[API Gateway]:::svc
272	    GW -->|"2 create trip MATCHING"| TS[Trip Service]:::svc
273	    TS -->|"3 persist trip"| TDB[(Trip Store)]:::store
274	    TS -->|"4 match request"| MQ[[Match Queue]]:::async
275	    MQ -->|"5 dequeue"| MS[Matching Service]:::svc
276	    MS -->|"6 read cell and neighbors"| GI[(Geo-Index Redis)]:::store
277	    MS -->|"7 atomic offer to driver"| GI
278	    MS -->|"8 offer created"| TS
279	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
280	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
281	    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
282	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
283	```
284	↳ reuses existing: API Gateway (from §6.1)
285	
286	**Numbered narration:**
287	1. Rider POSTs the trip with the estimateId + idempotency key. *State:* none yet. *What if double-tapped:* idempotency key returns the same trip.
288	2. Gateway routes to Trip Service. *Why Trip Service:* it's the single owner of trip state.
289	3. Trip Service writes a trip in `MATCHING`. *Why persist first:* if matching crashes, the trip still exists and a sweeper can retry. *State:* durable trip created. *What if DB write fails:* return 503; rider retries with same key.
290	4. Trip Service enqueues a match request. *Why a queue:* decouples the fast 202 ack from the few-second match; absorbs bursts. *State:* request buffered.
291	5. A Matching Service worker dequeues it. *Why a worker:* matching is CPU-light but I/O-bound on the index; scale workers independently.
292	6. Worker reads the pickup cell + 8 neighbors from the geo-index. *Why neighbors:* the nearest driver might be just across a cell edge. *State:* none (read). *What if cell is empty:* widen the radius (next ring of cells) before giving up.
293	7. Worker picks the best candidate and **atomically flips** them to `offered` (compare-and-set). *Why atomic:* prevents offering one driver to two riders. *State:* driver soft-locked. *What if the CAS loses:* try the next candidate.
294	8. Offer recorded; Trip Service is notified to create the MatchOffer. *State:* trip now has a pending offer.
295	
296	> 💬 "The whole trick of matching is turning 'who's near me' into 'who's in my cell and the eight around it' — a key lookup, not a database scan — and the offer is an atomic flip so no driver gets double-offered."
297	
298	> 🎙️ **Script:** "When a rider requests, the Trip Service creates a trip in MATCHING and persists it first — that way a crash can't lose the request — then drops a match job on a queue. A Matching worker reads the pickup's geo cell plus its eight neighbors from the in-memory index, which is fast because S2 cells turn proximity into a key lookup. It ranks the candidates and atomically flips the best one to 'offered' with a compare-and-set, so two riders can never grab the same driver. The trade-off is the index is best-effort and ephemeral, but for live positions that's exactly right — a stale ping just gets corrected in four seconds."
299	
300	### 6.3 "Driver accepts the offer; we lock the pairing"
301	
302	The offer is a soft lock with a timeout. This slice adds the Dispatch channel to the driver and the accept transition.
303	
304	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
305	|---|---|---|---|---|
306	| **Dispatch / Push channel** | A persistent connection (WebSocket) to the driver app to deliver offers instantly | Offers are time-sensitive (15s); push beats polling | Not polling (adds latency + load for an event-driven need) | Must manage long-lived connections + reconnects |
307	| **Offer timeout sweeper** | A background timer that expires unanswered offers and re-matches | Drivers miss offers; we must release the soft lock automatically | Not relying on the driver to decline (they may just ignore it) | A timer wheel / delayed queue to track expiries |
308	
309	> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
310	> **WebSocket** — a persistent two-way connection between app and server. Here it pushes the offer to the driver instantly and carries the accept back.
311	> **Soft lock with timeout** — a temporary reservation that auto-releases if not confirmed. Here it holds a driver for one rider for ~15s, then frees them.
312	
313	**Diagram — accept / re-match:**
314	```mermaid
315	flowchart TD
316	    MS[Matching Service]:::svc
317	    MS -->|"1 push offer"| DP[Dispatch Channel]:::svc
318	    DP -->|"2 deliver to driver"| D[Driver app]:::client
319	    D -->|"3 POST accept"| GW[API Gateway]:::svc
320	    GW -->|"4 atomic confirm"| TS[Trip Service]:::svc
321	    TS -->|"5 trip MATCHED"| TDB[(Trip Store)]:::store
322	    TS -->|"6 driver on-trip"| GI[(Geo-Index Redis)]:::store
323	    SW[Timeout Sweeper]:::async -.->|"7 expire if no accept"| MS
324	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
325	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
326	    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
327	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
328	```
329	↳ reuses existing: Matching Service, Trip Service, Geo-Index, Trip Store, API Gateway
330	
331	**Numbered narration:**
332	1. Matching pushes the offer to the Dispatch channel. *State:* offer pending, driver soft-locked.
333	2. Dispatch delivers to the driver's app over the live connection. *What if driver is offline/disconnected:* offer times out fast, re-match immediately.
334	3. Driver taps accept → POST. *Object authz:* JWT driver id must equal the offer target.
335	4. Gateway routes to Trip Service for an atomic confirm. *Why atomic:* the offer must still be PENDING and unexpired. *What if expired:* 409, driver told "offer expired."
336	5. Trip moves to `MATCHED`. *State:* durable pairing recorded.
337	6. Driver flips to `on-trip` in the index so no one else matches them. *State:* driver removed from the available pool.
338	7. If no accept within ~15s, the sweeper expires the offer and triggers a re-match. *Why:* prevents a rider waiting forever on a silent driver.
339	
340	> 💬 "An offer is a reservation with a fuse — if the driver doesn't accept in fifteen seconds it auto-releases and we re-match, so nobody's stuck."
341	
342	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The offer goes to the driver over a persistent push connection because fifteen seconds matters. They accept, and the Trip Service does an atomic confirm — only valid if the offer's still pending — then marks the trip MATCHED and flips the driver to on-trip so they leave the matchable pool. The piece people forget is the timeout sweeper: if the driver ignores the offer, a background timer releases the soft lock and re-matches, so a silent driver never strands a rider."
343	
344	### 6.4 "Both track the trip live on a map"
345	
346	Now the firehose becomes useful: the driver's position streams to the one rider watching. New component: the Location Ingest path + the track fan-out.
347	
348	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
349	|---|---|---|---|---|
350	| **Location Ingest Service** | Stateless service that takes the driver ping firehose, updates the index, and emits to the track stream | Decouples the write firehose from everything else | Not writing pings straight to the trip DB (would melt it) | One extra hop on each ping (cheap, stateless) |
351	| **Kafka (location stream)** | A durable log that buffers location events for downstream consumers | Lets analytics, ETA refresh, and history consume pings without touching the hot path | Not direct calls to each consumer (couples producer to all) | Pings in Kafka are best-effort-durable, async |
352	| **Track Pub/Sub** | A channel keyed by trip that pushes the matched driver's position to the rider | Fan-out of one driver to exactly one rider watching | Not broadcasting all positions to all clients (privacy + waste) | Connection state per active trip |
353	
354	> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
355	> **Kafka** — a durable, append-only log that buffers a firehose of events so slow consumers don't lose data. Here it carries location pings to history, ETA, and analytics off the hot path.
356	> **Pub/Sub** — publish/subscribe: producers publish to a topic, subscribers get the messages. Here the rider subscribes to their trip's topic to receive the driver's live position.
357	
358	**Diagram — live track:**
359	```mermaid
360	flowchart TD
361	    D[Driver app]:::client
362	    D -->|"1 location pings"| GW[API Gateway]:::svc
363	    GW -->|"2 forward firehose"| LI[Location Ingest]:::svc
364	    LI -->|"3 update live position"| GI[(Geo-Index Redis)]:::store
365	    LI -->|"4 emit event"| KF[[Kafka location stream]]:::async
366	    LI -->|"5 push to trip topic"| PS[Track PubSub]:::svc
367	    PS -->|"6 stream to rider"| R[Rider app]:::client
368	    KF -.->|"7 consume async"| HIST[(Location History)]:::store
369	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
370	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
371	    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
372	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
373	```
374	↳ reuses existing: API Gateway, Geo-Index
375	
376	**Numbered narration:**
377	1. Driver app streams pings (every ~4s). *State:* none durable.
378	2. Gateway forwards to Location Ingest. *Why a dedicated service:* isolate the firehose so it can't drag down matching or trips.
379	3. Ingest overwrites the driver's position in the geo-index. *Why overwrite:* only the latest matters for matching/tracking. *What if a ping is lost:* next one arrives in 4s — within our 5s freshness SLO.
380	4. Ingest emits the event to Kafka. *Why:* history, ETA refresh, and analytics consume it without touching the hot path. *What if Kafka is slow:* ingest keeps serving live updates; only the async consumers lag.
381	5. For drivers on an active trip, Ingest pushes to that trip's track topic. *State:* none durable; transient delivery.
382	6. Rider's open stream receives the position; map updates. *What if rider disconnected:* reconnect resumes from last known position.
383	7. Kafka consumers persist breadcrumbs to Location History (the ~58 TB/mo table) asynchronously.
384	
385	> 💬 "Tracking is just the firehose, filtered: the driver's ping updates the index for matching AND gets pushed to the one rider watching this trip — same event, two jobs."
386	
387	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The driver's location pings hit a dedicated Ingest service so the firehose is quarantined from everything correctness-critical. Ingest does three things with each ping: overwrites the live position in Redis for matching, emits it to Kafka for history and ETA, and — if the driver's on a trip — pushes it to that trip's topic so the rider's map moves. Losing a ping is a non-event because another comes in four seconds, which is inside my freshness target."
388	
389	### 6.5 "Compute the fare and charge the rider"
390	
391	The trip ends; we compute the real fare from the actual route and charge exactly once. New component: the Payment Service + the saga.
392	
393	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
394	|---|---|---|---|---|
395	| **Payment Service** | Owns charging the rider and paying the driver, idempotently | Money needs an isolated owner with strong consistency + idempotency | Not charging inside Trip Service (mixes concerns, harder to audit) | A hop + a saga between trip-complete and paid |
396	| **Trip Store (SQL, sharded by trip_id)** | The durable trip + fare record with transactions | A finished trip + fare is a row that must commit atomically | Not Cassandra for the trip (no strong multi-field txn for money) | Manual sharding by trip_id |
397	| **Payment processor** | External (Stripe) that does the actual card charge | We don't want PCI scope; processors are battle-tested | Not handling cards ourselves (PCI nightmare, liability) | External latency + occasional outages to handle |
398	
399	> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
400	> **Saga** — a sequence of steps with compensating undo actions, used instead of one big distributed transaction. Here it coordinates trip-complete → charge → payout so a failure can be cleanly undone.
401	> **Idempotency key** — a unique token (here the trip id) that makes a repeated request produce the same single result. Here it guarantees a retry never double-charges.
402	
403	**Diagram — fare + payment:**
404	```mermaid
405	flowchart TD
406	    D[Driver app]:::client
407	    D -->|"1 POST complete"| GW[API Gateway]:::svc
408	    GW -->|"2 finalize trip"| TS[Trip Service]:::svc
409	    TS -->|"3 compute fare from route"| RT[Routing ETA Service]:::ext
410	    TS -->|"4 trip COMPLETED + fare"| TDB[(Trip Store)]:::store
411	    TS -->|"5 charge event"| PAY[Payment Service]:::svc
412	    PAY -->|"6 charge idempotent on tripId"| PROC[Payment Processor]:::ext
413	    PAY -->|"7 record payment"| TDB
414	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
415	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
416	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
417	    classDef ext fill:#241a2e,stroke:#a78bfa,stroke-width:2px,color:#ede9fe
418	```
419	↳ reuses existing: API Gateway, Trip Service, Trip Store, Routing/ETA
420	
421	**Numbered narration:**
422	1. Driver marks the trip complete. *State:* trip ending.
423	2. Trip Service finalizes. *Why it owns this:* it's the trip's authoritative state machine.
424	3. It computes the real fare from the actual driven route (distance + time + surge locked at request). *Why server-side:* the fare must be derived, never client-claimed.
425	4. Trip moves to `COMPLETED` with the fare, committed atomically. *State:* durable, billable.
426	5. Trip Service emits a charge event to Payment. *Why async/saga:* the processor can be slow or down; don't block trip completion on it.
427	6. Payment charges the processor **idempotently on tripId**. *What if it times out:* retry with the same key — the processor returns the original charge, never a second.
428	7. Payment records the result. *What if charge fails (declined):* mark trip `PAYMENT_FAILED`, retry/dunning later — the ride still completed.
429	
430	> 💬 "Money is the one place I refuse to be eventual: the fare's computed server-side from the real route, and the charge is keyed on trip id so a retry is the same charge, never a double."
431	
432	> 🎙️ **Script:** "When the driver completes, the Trip Service computes the fare from the actual route — never trusting any client number — commits the trip as COMPLETED, and hands a charge event to a dedicated Payment Service. Payment charges the external processor idempotently on the trip id, so if the network hiccups and we retry, the processor returns the same charge instead of billing twice. I run this as a saga rather than a distributed transaction because the processor is external — I can't hold a lock across it — so on a decline I mark the trip payment-failed and reconcile, without ever holding up trip completion."

482	
483	### PART A — Entity table
484	
485	| Entity | Key fields | Chosen store | Shard/partition key | Consistency |
486	|---|---|---|---|---|
487	| Rider | **rider_id (PK)**, name, payment_method_token, active_trip_id | SQL (sharded) | rider_id | Strong |
488	| Driver | **driver_id (PK)**, vehicle, status, rating | SQL (sharded) | driver_id | Strong |
489	| DriverLocation | **driver_id (key)**, geo_cell, lat, lng, heading, status, ts | Redis (geo-sharded) | geo_cell prefix | Best-effort, last-write-wins |
490	| Trip | **trip_id (PK)**, rider_id, driver_id, status, fare, route_summary, ts_history | SQL (sharded) | trip_id | Strong |
491	| MatchOffer | **offer_id (PK)**, trip_id, driver_id, status, expires_at | Redis (TTL) | trip_id | Strong (atomic CAS) |
492	| Payment | **payment_id (PK)**, trip_id (idempotency key), amount, status | SQL (sharded) | trip_id | Strong |
493	| LocationHistory | **(trip_id, ts) (PK)**, driver_id, lat, lng | Object store + OLAP, time-partitioned | day partition | Eventual |
494	
495	### PART B — Storage decision cards
496	
497	**🗄️ Redis (in-memory) — used for: DriverLocation (Geo-Index), Surge Store, MatchOffer**
498	1. **What it is:** Redis is an in-memory key-value store — data lives in RAM so reads and writes are sub-millisecond, with optional geospatial commands.
499	2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern that forces it:** the location firehose is ~1.1M writes/s with sub-30ms "who's near me" reads; only RAM survives that throughput, and ~540 MB of live positions fits comfortably. Its geo/sorted-set support makes cell+neighbor lookups native, and atomic CAS makes the offer soft-lock safe.
500	3. **What we considered and rejected:**
501	   - **Postgres + PostGIS** — beautiful geo queries, but a single node can't take a million writes/s; we'd be sharding it into oblivion and still fighting disk.
502	   - **Cassandra/DynamoDB** — great write throughput, but a disk LSM-tree write per ping is wasteful when we only ever need the latest, and proximity reads aren't native.
503	   - **Elasticsearch geo** — query-rich but ingest can't keep up with this write rate, and it's heavier to operate.
504	4. **Trade-off we accept:** the index is ephemeral — a Redis failover can lose recent positions, but drivers re-ping within 4s so the index self-heals.
505	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Live driver positions go in Redis, in memory, because we're doing about a million writes a second and we only ever care about the latest position. Postgres can't take that write rate, and a disk database is wasteful when every value is overwritten in four seconds. The risk is a failover loses some positions, but they come right back from the next ping."
506	
507	**🗄️ SQL (sharded Postgres) — used for: Rider, Driver, Trip, Payment**
508	1. **What it is:** a relational database that stores rows with strict schemas and guarantees a multi-step operation either fully commits or fully rolls back (ACID transactions).
509	2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern that forces it:** money and trip state must be strongly consistent and atomic — completing a trip and recording its fare can't half-commit, and a payment must be exactly-once keyed on trip_id. Only a transactional relational store gives that cleanly.
510	3. **What we considered and rejected:**
511	   - **DynamoDB** — fine for simple KV, but the trip+fare+payment lifecycle wants multi-row transactions and rich queries Dynamo makes painful.
512	   - **MongoDB** — flexible schema, but its transactions are less battle-tested for money than Postgres.
513	   - **MySQL** — essentially equivalent; Postgres is the modern default with better JSON and partitioning.
514	4. **Trade-off we accept:** manual sharding by trip_id; cross-shard joins are impossible so we denormalize the rider/driver summary onto the trip.
515	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Trips and payments go in sharded Postgres because it's money — I need atomic commits and exactly-once charging. Dynamo's great for the geo-index but I don't want to hand-roll transactional logic for the money path. The cost is I shard by trip id and can't do cross-shard joins, so I denormalize."
516	
517	**🗄️ Kafka — used for: location event stream (feeds history, ETA refresh, analytics, surge)**
518	1. **What it is:** a durable, append-only log that buffers a firehose of events so multiple consumers can read at their own pace without losing data.
519	2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern that forces it:** location events have many independent consumers (history, surge, ETA, fraud, analytics) and we must decouple them from the hot path; Kafka fans one stream out durably.
520	3. **What we considered and rejected:**
521	   - **Direct service calls** — couples the producer to every consumer and loses events if one is down.
522	   - **AWS Kinesis / Pub/Sub** — viable managed equivalents; Kafka chosen for partition control and ecosystem maturity.
523	4. **Trade-off we accept:** another system to operate; events are async so consumers can lag.
524	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "I put location events on Kafka so history, surge, and analytics each consume the firehose at their own speed without touching the live path. If one consumer falls behind, the log holds the events; it doesn't take down matching."
525	
526	**🗄️ Object store + OLAP (S3 + warehouse) — used for: LocationHistory**
527	1. **What it is:** cheap, durable blob storage (S3) plus an analytics warehouse for querying it.
528	2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern that forces it:** ~58 TB/mo of breadcrumbs are write-once, read-rarely (disputes, routing improvement, analytics) — cheap durable storage beats keeping it in an operational DB.
529	3. **What we considered and rejected:**
530	   - **Keeping it in Postgres/Redis** — orders of magnitude too expensive and would crush the operational store.
531	4. **Trade-off we accept:** querying history is slow (warehouse-speed), which is fine — it's not on any hot path.
532	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Raw GPS history is sixty terabytes a month, written once and read almost never, so it goes to cheap object storage with a warehouse on top for the occasional dispute or analytics query."
533	
534	### PART C — Per-operation consistency
535	
536	| Operation | Store | Consistency | Why that level |
537	|---|---|---|---|
538	| Create trip / accept offer | SQL + Redis CAS | Strong | A driver can't be double-booked; trip state must be authoritative |
539	| Charge payment | SQL | Strong, exactly-once | Money — idempotent on trip_id |
540	| Read driver position (track) | Redis | Eventual (≤5s) | A few seconds stale is invisible and fine |
541	| Read surge for estimate | Redis | Eventual (seconds) | Market drifts; a slightly old multiplier is acceptable |
542	| **Read-your-own-writes: rider sees their new trip** | SQL primary | Strong | After POST /trips returns, the track stream reads trip from primary (not a lagging replica) so the rider always sees MATCHING immediately |
543	
544	### PART D — Data lifecycle (LocationHistory, the biggest table)
545	
546	At ~1.9 TB/day of breadcrumbs, a 90-day retention window caps us at ~175 TB steady-state — without it we'd grow unbounded. We store it **time-partitioned by day**, and reclaim space with a **partition DROP** (an O(1) instant operation that removes a whole day's table) rather than row-by-row DELETE (which churns indexes and never cleanly reclaims space). Data older than 90 days that must be kept for compliance is rolled to **Glacier-tier cold storage**; everything else is dropped.
547	
548	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Storage splits cleanly by guarantee. The money and trip data is in sharded Postgres for atomic, exactly-once correctness — I considered Dynamo but didn't want to hand-roll transactional logic for charges. Live positions are in Redis because we're doing a million writes a second on data we overwrite constantly. Kafka carries the location firehose to all the async consumers so nothing slow touches the hot path. And the big sixty-terabyte breadcrumb history sits in cheap object storage with day-partitions I just drop after ninety days."
549

552	## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great
553	
554	> 🆘 **If you get stuck:** go back to the bottleneck — "a million location writes a second, fastest in the densest cities" — and reason forward from there.
555	
556	### How do we absorb the location-update write firehose (~1.1M writes/s)?
557	
558	**#### Bad: write every ping to Postgres**
559	- 🗣️ *Plain words:* just store each GPS update as a row in the main database.
560	- **Approach:** every ping is an `UPDATE driver_location SET lat=...` in Postgres.
561	- *Why people try it:* it's one store, one mental model.
562	- ⚠️ *What breaks:* at ~1.1M writes/s, a Postgres node handling maybe ~10-20K writes/s is overwhelmed 50-100x over; the WAL and connection pool saturate in seconds and the whole DB — including trips and payments — goes down with it.
563	- 🔁 *Forces upgrade:* the firehose volume itself; we need an in-memory store, not disk.
564	
565	**#### Good: in-memory geo-index sharded by cell**
566	- ↩️ *What the previous tier got wrong:* Bad put a million disk writes/s on a transactional DB. Good moves live positions to RAM and overwrites instead of appending.
567	- 🗣️ *Plain words:* keep every driver's current spot in fast memory, bucketed by map cell, and just overwrite it each ping.
568	- **Approach:** a Redis cluster keyed by **geo cell** (S2/geohash). Each ping is a key overwrite (last-write-wins). We **geo-shard**: cell-prefix → shard, so ~11+ shards each handle ~100K writes/s. Pings are best-effort — no durability on the hot path; they're mirrored to Kafka async for history.
569	- ⚠️ *What breaks:* a single dense downtown cell pins one shard — ~500 writes/s plus the read-scan load all on one node — so a uniform-hash assumption is wrong; traffic is geographically lumpy.
570	- 🔁 *Forces upgrade:* the hot dense-city cell — one shard becomes a hotspot for both writes and the 18K-row read scan.
571	
572	**Mechanism diagram (Good):**
573	```mermaid
574	flowchart TD
575	    D[Driver pings]:::client
576	    D -->|"1 ping by cell"| LI[Location Ingest]:::svc
577	    LI -->|"2 hash cell to shard"| RT{Shard router}:::svc
578	    RT -->|"3 cell A"| S1[(Geo shard 1)]:::store
579	    RT -->|"4 cell B"| S2[(Geo shard 2)]:::store
580	    RT -->|"5 cell C"| S3[(Geo shard N)]:::store
581	    LI -.->|"6 async mirror"| KF[[Kafka]]:::async
582	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
583	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
584	    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
585	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
586	```
587	
588	**#### Great: adaptive cell sizing + sub-sharding hot cells**
589	- ↩️ *What the previous tier got wrong:* Good used fixed-size cells, so a dense cell overloads one shard while a rural cell is nearly empty.
590	- 🗣️ *Plain words:* make the map grid finer where it's crowded and coarser where it's empty, and split a hot cell across several machines.
591	- **Approach:** use **hierarchical cells** (S2 levels) — downtown Manhattan splits into many small cells, rural areas use one big cell. A cell that exceeds a write/read threshold is **sub-sharded** (its drivers spread across replicas by a secondary key). Reads scan the sub-shards in parallel and merge top-K. Cell sizing adapts to driver density, rebalanced periodically.
592	- 🔢 *Decision math:* a fixed level-13 cell downtown = ~2,000 drivers, ~500 writes/s + 18K-row scans on ONE node. Splitting into 8 finer cells = ~250 drivers and ~60 writes/s each — back under any single-node concern.
593	- ✅ **Failure matrix:**
594	
595	| Scenario | What happens |
596	|---|---|
597	| Shard node dies | Drivers in that cell re-ping within 4s; index rebuilds; matching briefly widens radius |
598	| Hot cell overloads | Auto sub-shard triggered at threshold; reads fan out and merge |
599	| Ping lost | Next ping (≤4s) corrects; within freshness SLO |
600	| Rebalance mid-traffic | Dual-read old+new cell during cutover so no driver is invisible |
601	| Whole region down | Failover to standby region; pings re-establish in seconds |
602	
603	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The firehose is the whole ballgame — a million writes a second. I refuse to put that on a disk database; it goes into an in-memory Redis index keyed by geo cell, where each ping just overwrites the last because I only care about the latest position. I shard by cell so the load spreads, but the catch is cities are lumpy — one downtown cell can pin a single shard. So I use adaptive cell sizes — fine grid where it's dense, coarse where it's empty — and I sub-shard any cell that gets too hot, fanning the read scan across nodes and merging the top candidates."
604	
605	> 🧠 *If they ask "why not just buy bigger Redis nodes?":* "Vertical scaling tops out and doesn't fix the hotspot — one dense cell is on one node no matter how big. Geographic sharding plus sub-sharding hot cells is the only thing that spreads a lumpy load."
606	
607	### How do we match efficiently in a hot dense city (the celebrity cell)?
608	
609	**#### Good: scan cell + neighbors, rank top-K**
610	- 🗣️ *Plain words:* look at the requester's cell and the eight around it, score the drivers, pick the best.
611	- **Approach:** read candidate drivers from the 9 cells, score by ETA (not straight-line distance), offer the top one.
612	- ⚠️ *What breaks:* downtown, those 9 cells hold ~18K drivers — scanning and scoring all of them per request, with ~100/s match requests funneling to that one cell-shard, makes the dense city the bottleneck.
613	- 🔁 *Forces upgrade:* the read-scan cost + concurrent matchers contending on the same hot cell.
614	
615	**#### Great: bounded top-K with concurrent-offer guarding**
616	- ↩️ *What the previous tier got wrong:* Good scanned all 18K and let concurrent matchers fight over the same drivers.
617	- 🗣️ *Plain words:* keep drivers pre-sorted by location so we grab just the nearest handful, and make sure two simultaneous requests don't both grab the same driver.
618	- **Approach:** maintain drivers in a **sorted geo structure** (Redis GEO sorted-set) so we read only the nearest ~20 candidates, not all 18K. Each offer is an **atomic CAS** flipping the driver to `offered`; a concurrent matcher that loses the CAS moves to the next candidate. Sub-shard the hot cell (from the firehose dive) so the scans parallelize.
619	- 🔢 *Decision math:* scanning 20 sorted candidates vs 18,000 = ~900x less work per match; at 100 matches/s on the hot cell that's the difference between feasible and melted.
620	- ✅ **Failure matrix:**
621	
622	| Scenario | What happens |
623	|---|---|
624	| Two riders, one driver | First CAS wins; second matcher skips to next candidate |
625	| All nearby drivers busy | Widen to next ring of cells, then queue rider with surge nudge |
626	| Driver goes offline mid-offer | Offer times out; sweeper re-matches |
627	
628	> 🎙️ **Script:** "In a dense city, naively scanning every nearby driver is thousands of rows per request. Instead I keep drivers in a sorted-by-distance structure so I only pull the nearest twenty. The other half is concurrency: two requests downtown might eye the same driver, so the offer is an atomic compare-and-set — first one wins, the loser just takes the next-best. Combined with sub-sharding the hot cell, the densest block stays fast."
629	
630	> 🧠 *If they ask "why ETA not distance?":* "Straight-line distance ignores a river or a one-way street — the closest car as the crow flies might be 15 minutes away. I score by routing ETA so the offer is actually the soonest pickup."
631	
632	### How do we keep payments exactly-once across a crash?
633	
634	**#### Bad: charge synchronously inside trip completion**
635	- 🗣️ *Plain words:* charge the card in the same step that ends the trip.
636	- ⚠️ *What breaks:* if the processor times out, we don't know if it charged; a retry might double-bill, and holding the trip transaction open across a 300ms external call exhausts connections.
637	- 🔁 *Forces upgrade:* the external call can't be inside a DB transaction.
638	
639	**#### Great: saga with idempotency key = trip_id**
640	- ↩️ *What the previous tier got wrong:* Bad coupled the commit to an external charge it can't control.
641	- 🗣️ *Plain words:* finish the trip first, then charge separately with a unique receipt number so a retry can't double-bill.
642	- **Approach:** trip completion commits in Postgres independently. A **saga** then charges the processor with `tripId` as the **idempotency key** — the processor dedupes, so a retry returns the original charge. On decline, mark `PAYMENT_FAILED` and run dunning; never re-run the trip.
643	- 🔢 *Decision math:* with synchronous charging, 1K completions/s × 300ms processor latency = 300 in-flight DB transactions just waiting on the network — pool exhaustion. The saga frees the DB the instant the trip commits.
644	- ✅ **Failure matrix:**
645	
646	| Scenario | What happens |
647	|---|---|
648	| Charge succeeds, our record crashes | Reconcile by trip_id; processor shows charged; we record it; no double-charge |
649	| Charge times out | Retry same key; processor returns original result |
650	| Card declined | Trip stays COMPLETED, payment PAYMENT_FAILED, dunning retries |
651	| Duplicate complete event | Idempotent on trip_id; second is a no-op |
652	
653	> 🎙️ **Script:** "I never charge inside the trip transaction — the processor is external and slow. I commit the completed trip in Postgres, then a saga charges with the trip id as the idempotency key, so any retry returns the same charge instead of billing twice. If the card declines, the ride still completed; I just mark payment-failed and run dunning. And if our side crashes after the processor charged, I reconcile by trip id — the processor is the source of truth for whether money moved."
654	
655	> 🧠 *If they ask "why not two-phase commit?":* "We don't own the processor, so we can't make it a transaction participant. A saga with idempotency is the standard way to coordinate across a system you don't control."
656	
657	### How does surge pricing work (the demand/supply deep dive)?
658	
659	**#### Good: per-cell supply/demand ratio**
660	- 🗣️ *Plain words:* if a neighborhood has way more riders than drivers, raise the price there.
661	- **Approach:** per geo cell, compute `open_requests / available_drivers` over a short window; map the ratio to a multiplier; write it to the Surge Store. Estimates read it.
662	- ⚠️ *What breaks:* a raw instantaneous ratio is jumpy — surge flickers 1.0→2.5→1.2 minute to minute, which feels arbitrary and triggers gaming.
663	- 🔁 *Forces upgrade:* volatility and the feedback loop (high surge pulls drivers in, which should lower it smoothly).
664	
665	**#### Great: smoothed, hysteresis-banded surge with feedback**
666	- ↩️ *What the previous tier got wrong:* Good's raw ratio whipsawed riders and drivers.
667	- 🗣️ *Plain words:* average the demand over a rolling window and only change the price when it crosses clear bands, so it moves smoothly.
668	- **Approach:** an **exponentially-weighted moving average** of the supply/demand ratio per cell, mapped to multiplier **bands with hysteresis** (must cross a threshold to step up, a lower one to step back down) so it doesn't flicker. Drivers see a heatmap, pull toward surge, which naturally relaxes it — a closed feedback loop computed by a stream job consuming the Kafka location+request events.
669	- 🔢 *Decision math:* a 2-minute EWMA over events smooths a spike that a 10-second window would amplify; hysteresis bands (e.g. step up at 1.5x ratio, down at 1.2x) prevent oscillation around a boundary.
670	- ✅ **Failure matrix:**
671	
672	| Scenario | What happens |
673	|---|---|
674	| Surge pipeline lags | Estimates use last-known multiplier (stale-but-safe), default 1.0 if absent |
675	| Sudden event (concert lets out) | EWMA ramps over ~2 min, not instant spike; bands cap the jump |
676	| Gaming (drivers log off to spike) | Smoothing + minimum-supply floor dampens manipulation |
677	
678	**Mechanism diagram (Great):**
679	```mermaid
680	flowchart TD
681	    KF[[Kafka events]]:::async
682	    KF -->|"1 requests and supply"| SJ[Surge Stream Job]:::svc
683	    SJ -->|"2 EWMA per cell"| CALC[Banded multiplier]:::svc
684	    CALC -->|"3 write multiplier"| SG[(Surge Store)]:::store
685	    SG -->|"4 read at estimate"| EST[Estimate Service]:::svc
686	    CALC -.->|"5 heatmap"| D[Driver app]:::client
687	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
688	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
689	    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
690	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
691	```
692	
693	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Surge is just per-cell supply versus demand, but the naive ratio flickers and feels arbitrary. So a stream job consumes the request and location events from Kafka, keeps a smoothed moving average per cell, and maps it to multiplier bands with hysteresis so the price only steps when demand clearly crosses a threshold. Drivers see a heatmap and drift toward the surge, which relaxes it — a feedback loop. If the pipeline lags, estimates just use the last-known multiplier, defaulting to no surge, so we fail safe on price."
694	
695	> 🧠 *If they ask "what if surge is wrong/stale?":* "It's eventually consistent by design — a few seconds stale is fine, and we pin the rider's quote with an estimate id so whatever they saw is what they pay, even if it changes a second later."
696	
697	### How do we handle back-pressure when the system is overloaded?
698	
699	**#### Great: bounded queues + load-shedding order**
700	- 🗣️ *Plain words:* when we're drowning, drop the least important work first and protect the money path.
701	- **Approach:** the Match Queue and Kafka are **bounded**; the trigger signal is **consumer lag / queue depth**. Under overload we shed in order: (1) downgrade location freshness (ping every 8s not 4s) → (2) reject new estimate reads with 429 → (3) shed non-critical analytics consumers → never shed trip completion or payment. Clients get **HTTP 429 with Retry-After**.
702	- 🔢 *Decision math:* doubling the ping interval halves the firehose from ~1.1M to ~550K writes/s — instant relief on the binding bottleneck without dropping any trip.
703	- ✅ **Failure matrix:**
704	
705	| Scenario | What happens |
706	|---|---|
707	| Match queue depth spikes | Shed reads first; widen ping interval; protect accepts/payments |
708	| Kafka consumer lag grows | History/analytics lag (acceptable); hot path unaffected |
709	| Total overload | Load-shed reads, return 429+Retry-After; money path stays up |
710	
711	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Under overload I shed work in a deliberate order. First I widen the GPS ping interval — going from four seconds to eight instantly halves the firehose, my biggest load, while the map's barely less fresh. Then I start rejecting estimate reads with a 429 and a Retry-After. The one thing I never shed is trip completion and payment — the money path is sacred. The queues are all bounded, and queue depth is the trigger, because an unbounded queue is just a slow-motion outage."
712	
713	> 🧠 *If they ask "why widen pings first?":* "It's the cheapest, least-visible relief — it attacks the single biggest load source and a rider can't tell the difference between a 4-second and 8-second map refresh."
714

717	## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost
718	
719	### 9A — Availability targets
720	
721	- **Matching path: 99.9% ("three nines")** = ~8.7 hours/year max downtime. Mechanisms: stateless services behind a load balancer, **multi-AZ** *(multiple data centers in one region so if one building loses power another takes over automatically)*, Redis with replicas + fast failover. We accept three nines here because a brief matching blip is recoverable — the rider retries.
722	- **Payment/trip path: 99.99% ("four nines")** = ~52 min/year. Mechanisms: synchronously-replicated Postgres (RPO≈0), idempotent charging so retries are safe, multi-AZ with automatic failover.
723	- **Location track: best-effort** — a few seconds of staleness is acceptable; no strict nines because a lost ping self-corrects.
724	
725	### 9B — Per-dependency failure table
726	
727	| Component | What breaks if down | Graceful degradation (what the USER sees) | Recovery |
728	|---|---|---|---|
729	| Geo-Index (Redis) | Can't match / track | Matching pauses briefly; riders see "finding a driver"; drivers re-ping and index rebuilds in seconds | Failover to replica; index rebuilt from incoming pings |
730	| Trip Store (Postgres) | Can't create/complete trips | New requests fail (503, retry); in-flight trips tracked from cache; money frozen, not lost | Multi-AZ failover, synchronous replica promoted |
731	| Matching Service | No new matches | Riders wait in MATCHING; existing trips track fine | Stateless — restart/scale replicas |
732	| Payment processor | Can't charge | Trip completes normally; charge queued and retried later | Saga retries; dunning; reconcile by trip_id |
733	| Routing/ETA | No fresh ETAs | Estimates use cached/region-average ETA; quote flagged approximate | Circuit-breaker + cache; auto-recover |
734	| Kafka | History/surge/analytics lag | Live matching & tracking unaffected; surge uses last-known | Consumers catch up from the durable log |
735	
736	### 9C — RPO / RTO per data class
737	
738	> **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** = how much data we can lose if it crashes right now. RPO≈0 means none — every write is replicated before we acknowledge. **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** = how long until service is back.
739	
740	| Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism |
741	|---|---|---|---|
742	| Payments / Trips | ≈0 (no loss) | < 1 min | Synchronous multi-AZ replication; auto-failover |
743	| DriverLocation (live) | seconds (lossy OK) | seconds | Self-heals from next ping; no DR needed |
744	| LocationHistory | minutes (rebuildable) | hours OK | Kafka replay re-populates; cold tier durable |
745	| Surge state | seconds (recomputed) | seconds | Stream job recomputes from Kafka |
746	
747	### 9D — Cost breakdown
748	
749	Top 3 drivers:
750	1. **The location write tier (Redis cluster + Kafka + ingest):** ~$200K/mo — the firehose dominates, working out to **~$0.21 per 1M location updates**. This, not the trip DB, is the biggest line item.
751	2. **Location-history storage + warehouse:** ~58 TB/mo of breadcrumbs; storage itself is cheap (~$2K/mo on object store) but the warehouse compute for queries adds up.
752	3. **Egress / push connections:** millions of long-lived track + dispatch connections cost in bandwidth and connection-handling fleet.
753	
754	**Biggest optimization:** the ping interval. Widening from 4s to 8s roughly halves the firehose — the single largest cost — at a barely-perceptible freshness cost. Adaptive ping rate (fast when on a trip, slow when idle) captures most of the savings without hurting active rides.
755	
756	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Here's what users actually feel when things break. If the geo-index hiccups, riders briefly see 'finding your driver' and drivers re-ping their way back in seconds — nothing lost. If Postgres fails over, in-flight trips keep tracking from cache and money is frozen, never lost, because payments are synchronously replicated at RPO zero. If the payment processor is down, the ride still ends normally and we charge a bit later via the saga. The one thing that's expensive is the firehose itself — about twenty cents per million pings — and the cheapest big win is simply pinging less often when a driver's idle."
757
