760	## 10. Trade-off Ledger
761	
762	**🔀 Decision: in-memory best-effort geo-index vs durable geospatial DB**
763	1. **Chose & why:** Redis in RAM — only thing that absorbs ~1.1M writes/s with sub-30ms reads.
764	2. **Gave up:** durability of live positions — a failover can lose recent pings.
765	3. 🗣️ *Plain words:* like a whiteboard you wipe and rewrite constantly instead of a logbook — fast, but it doesn't remember history.
766	4. **Reverses when:** if regulators require every breadcrumb durably on the hot path, I'd switch to a log-structured durable ingest and ~5-10x the write-tier cost.
767	5. 🗣️ *How to say it:* "Live positions are disposable, so I keep them in fast memory and let a lost ping self-correct in four seconds."
768	
769	**🔀 Decision: saga with idempotency vs distributed transaction for payment**
770	1. **Chose & why:** saga — we don't own the external processor, can't make it a transaction participant.
771	2. **Gave up:** the simplicity of a single atomic commit; we need reconciliation logic.
772	3. 🗣️ *Plain words:* like checking out at a store where the card machine is a separate company — you finalize the receipt, then run the card, and reconcile if the machine hiccups.
773	4. **Reverses when:** if we owned payments in-house, a real two-phase commit becomes possible.
774	5. 🗣️ *How to say it:* "I charge after committing the trip, keyed on trip id, so a retry can't double-bill and I never hold a lock across an external call."
775	
776	**🔀 Decision: best-effort eventual track vs strongly-consistent location**
777	1. **Chose & why:** eventual (≤5s) — a near-live map is indistinguishable from live and infinitely cheaper.
778	2. **Gave up:** perfectly real-time positions.
779	3. 🗣️ *Plain words:* the car on your map is a couple seconds behind reality — which you'd never notice.
780	4. **Reverses when:** never realistically; sub-second would multiply cost for no user benefit.
781	5. 🗣️ *How to say it:* "Tracking is eventual on purpose — five seconds stale is invisible, and chasing real-time would blow the budget."
782	
783	**CAP/PACELC framing:** The location/matching path is **AP** — under a partition I favor availability (serve possibly-stale positions, keep matching) over consistency. The payment/trip path is **CP** — I'd rather reject a write than risk a double-charge. PACELC: even without a partition, the location path favors **latency** over consistency, while the money path favors **consistency**.
784	
785	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The three decisions I'd defend hardest: live positions in memory and best-effort, because the firehose makes durability there absurdly expensive and a lost ping self-heals; a payment saga instead of a distributed transaction, because we don't own the processor; and eventual tracking, because nobody can tell a five-second-old map from a live one. The thread through all of them is the same — I'm AP and latency-first on the real-time geo path, and CP and consistency-first on money. The location decision reverses if regulators force durable breadcrumbs; the saga reverses if we bring payments in-house."
786	
787	---
788	
789	## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers
790	
791	**❓ "Two riders request at the same time and the nearest driver is the same person — who gets them?"**
792	*Mechanism:* matching offers a driver by an atomic compare-and-set (CAS) that flips them from `available` to `offered`; the first request wins the CAS, the second sees the driver already offered and moves to the next candidate.
793	🗣️ *Plain words:* whoever's request grabs the driver first locks them; the other request just picks the next-closest car.
794	💬 "Atomic CAS on the driver status — first to flip wins, no double-booking."
795	
796	**❓ "What happens when Redis (the geo-index) crashes?"**
797	*Mechanism:* matching pauses momentarily; we fail over to a replica, and because every online driver pings every 4 seconds, the index rebuilds itself within seconds from incoming pings — we never persisted it as the source of truth.
798	🗣️ *Plain words:* the live map briefly goes quiet, then refills on its own as cars keep reporting in.
799	💬 "The index self-heals — drivers re-ping it back to life in seconds."
800	
801	**❓ "How do you handle a flash crowd — a concert lets out and 10,000 people request at once?"**
802	*Mechanism:* the match queue absorbs the burst, the dense cell auto-sub-shards so the read scans parallelize, and surge ramps via a smoothed EWMA to pull in nearby drivers; under extreme load we widen ping intervals and shed estimate reads with 429s, never trip/payment.
803	🗣️ *Plain words:* we queue the spike, spread the hot neighborhood across more machines, and let surge draw drivers over.
804	💬 "Bounded queue + hot-cell sub-sharding + surge feedback — shed reads, protect rides."
805	
806	**❓ "Can the same rider be charged twice for one trip?"**
807	*Mechanism:* the charge is idempotent on trip_id — the payment processor dedupes by that key, so a retry returns the original charge rather than creating a second.
808	🗣️ *Plain words:* the trip id is the receipt number; charging twice with the same number just returns the first charge.
809	💬 "Idempotency key = trip id, so a retry is the same charge, never a double."
810	
811	**❓ "Can a rider see their trip immediately after requesting (read-your-own-writes)?"**
812	*Mechanism:* after POST /trips commits, the track stream reads the trip from the SQL primary (not a lagging replica), so the rider always sees `MATCHING` right away.
813	🗣️ *Plain words:* right after you tap request, we read your trip from the authoritative copy so you instantly see "finding a driver."
814	💬 "Read from primary right after the write — no replica-lag confusion."
815	
816	**❓ "What if a driver's phone loses signal in a tunnel mid-trip?"**
817	*Mechanism:* the live position simply stops updating; the map shows the last known spot and an estimate; when signal returns the next ping snaps it forward — the trip state machine is unaffected because trip state lives in Postgres, not in the ping.
818	🗣️ *Plain words:* the car freezes on the map for a moment, then jumps to its real spot when signal's back; the trip itself never breaks.
819	💬 "Pings are best-effort; the trip lives in the DB, so signal loss is cosmetic."
820	
821	**❓ "How do you stop someone from hacking a cheaper fare?"**
822	*Mechanism:* the client never sends a price — the server computes the fare from the actual route and the surge pinned by the estimate id; we re-validate the estimate server-side and ignore any client-supplied amount.
823	🗣️ *Plain words:* the price comes from our servers based on the real trip, not from anything the app sends, so there's nothing to tamper with.
824	💬 "Price is server-derived from the route and pinned estimate — the body can't set it."
825	
826	**❓ "How do you make sure driver A can't accept driver B's offer or watch someone else's trip?"**
827	*Mechanism:* object-level authorization — we check the JWT's identity against the offer's target driver and the trip's rider/driver on every mutating and streaming endpoint.
828	🗣️ *Plain words:* we check not just that you're logged in, but that this specific offer or trip is actually yours.
829	💬 "Object-level authz on every endpoint — your id must own the resource."
830	
831	**❓ "What's the most expensive part of this system?"**
832	*Mechanism:* the location write tier — Redis cluster + Kafka + ingest absorbing ~1.1M writes/s — at about $0.21 per million pings, dwarfing the trip database.
833	🗣️ *Plain words:* it's not storing trips, it's the constant flood of GPS updates from every car.
834	💬 "The firehose, about twenty cents per million pings — way more than the trip DB."
835	
836	**❓ "How would you cut costs without hurting users?"**
837	*Mechanism:* adaptive ping rate — pinging every 8s when idle and 4s on a trip roughly halves the firehose, the dominant cost, with no perceptible freshness loss; partition-drop retention caps history growth.
838	🗣️ *Plain words:* have idle cars report in less often — you'd never notice, and it halves our biggest bill.
839	💬 "Adaptive ping intervals halve the firehose for a freshness drop nobody can see."
840	
841	**❓ "Why straight-line distance isn't good enough for matching — what do you use?"**
842	*Mechanism:* we rank candidates by routing ETA, not Euclidean distance, because a river or one-way street can make the geographically-closest car the slowest to arrive.
843	🗣️ *Plain words:* the closest car on the map might be across a river — we pick the one that can actually get to you fastest.
844	💬 "Rank by real ETA, not crow-flies distance."
845	
846	**❓ "How would you add UberPool (shared rides) later?"**
847	*Mechanism:* pooling makes matching a batched route-overlap optimization rather than greedy 1:1 — I'd add a windowed batch matcher that groups riders with compatible routes and assigns one driver to several, layered on the same geo-index.
848	🗣️ *Plain words:* instead of one rider one driver, we'd briefly wait, group people heading the same way, and send one car for all of them.
849	💬 "Add a windowed batch matcher on top of the geo-index — same index, different matching policy."
850	
851	**❓ "How do you prevent an offer from stranding a rider if the driver ignores it?"**
852	*Mechanism:* every offer is a soft lock with a ~15s TTL; a timeout sweeper expires unanswered offers, releases the driver, and triggers a re-match automatically.
853	🗣️ *Plain words:* if a driver doesn't answer in fifteen seconds, we free them up and find someone else.
854	💬 "Offers auto-expire in 15s and re-match — no silent driver strands a rider."
855	
856	**❓ "How do you make matching consistent across a region with multiple shards?"**
857	*Mechanism:* trips are region-local by assumption, and the geo-index is sharded by cell within a region; a request reads its cell + neighbors, which may live on a couple shards but always within one region — no cross-region coordination on the hot path.
858	🗣️ *Plain words:* a ride stays inside one region, and we only ever look at nearby cells, so there's no global coordination needed.
859	💬 "Region-local trips + cell-neighbor reads — no global coordinator on the match path."
860	
861	**❓ "What if the matching service crashes after creating the trip but before matching?"**
862	*Mechanism:* the trip was persisted in `MATCHING` first, and the match request sits durably on the queue; a stuck-trip sweeper re-enqueues any trip in MATCHING past a timeout, so no request is silently lost.
863	🗣️ *Plain words:* the trip's already saved and the match job's on a queue, so if a worker dies another picks it up.
864	💬 "Persist trip first, match job on a durable queue, sweeper retries stuck ones."
865	
866	> 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "A ride-sharing service is, in plain words, a system that finds you a nearby car fast, lets you watch it come, and charges you the right amount once. The design splits into two halves. The real-time half is a firehose of GPS pings — about a million writes a second — landing in an in-memory geo-index keyed by map cell, where 'who's near me' becomes a fast cell-plus-neighbors lookup; the same pings stream to the rider's map and to Kafka for history. The transaction half is the trip state machine and payments in sharded Postgres — the hardest problem there is charging exactly once across an external processor, which I solve with a saga keyed on the trip id so a retry never double-bills. The two halves connect at the geo-index: matching reads what the location ingest writes, and Kafka carries everything async to history and surge. Every hard decision traces back to one principle — keep the moving-data path in memory and best-effort for speed, and keep the money path in SQL and exactly-once for correctness."
867

7	  3. Geo-index memory footprint (fits in RAM => Redis, not disk).

15	    if n >= 1_000_000_000:

17	    if n >= 1_000_000:
18	        return f"~{n/1_000_000:.1f}M"
19	    if n >= 1_000:
20	        return f"~{n/1_000:.0f}K"
21	    return f"~{n:.0f}"
22	
23	def gb(b):

25	    if b >= 1e12:
26	        return f"~{b/1e12:.1f} TB"
27	    if b >= 1e9:
28	        return f"~{b/1e9:.0f} GB"
29	    if b >= 1e6:
30	        return f"~{b/1e6:.0f} MB"
31	    return f"~{b:.0f} B"

33	SEC_PER_DAY = 86_400
34	print("=" * 64)

47	BURST = 3                         # peak-to-average factor (Fri/Sat 6-9pm rush)

49	print(f"    online drivers avg = {num(ONLINE_DRIVERS_AVG)}, peak (x{BURST}) = {num(ONLINE_DRIVERS_PEAK)}")

52	PING_EVERY_SEC = 4                # each online driver pings GPS every 4s
53	loc_writes_avg = ONLINE_DRIVERS_AVG / PING_EVERY_SEC
54	loc_writes_peak = ONLINE_DRIVERS_PEAK / PING_EVERY_SEC
55	print("\n[1] LOCATION-UPDATE FIREHOSE  (4s ping interval)")
56	print(f"    avg  = {ONLINE_DRIVERS_AVG:,} / {PING_EVERY_SEC}s  = {loc_writes_avg:,.0f}/s  -> {num(loc_writes_avg)}/s")
57	print(f"    PEAK = {ONLINE_DRIVERS_PEAK:,} / {PING_EVERY_SEC}s = {loc_writes_peak:,.0f}/s -> {num(loc_writes_peak)}/s")

69	match_qps_peak = match_qps_avg * BURST
70	print("\n[2] MATCHING (per ride request, reads a geo cell + 8 neighbors)")

72	print(f"    requests PEAK = x{BURST} = {match_qps_peak:,.0f}/s -> {num(match_qps_peak)}/s")
73	
74	# Hot dense city: a single S2 cell at level ~13 (~1km^2) downtown can hold a huge
75	# share of drivers. Manhattan-style cell at evening rush.
76	DRIVERS_IN_HOT_CELL = 2_000     # drivers parked/cruising in one dense downtown cell
77	NEIGHBOR_CELLS = 9              # cell + 8 neighbors scanned per match

607	### How do we match efficiently in a hot dense city (the celebrity cell)?
608	
609	**#### Good: scan cell + neighbors, rank top-K**
610	- 🗣️ *Plain words:* look at the requester's cell and the eight around it, score the drivers, pick the best.
611	- **Approach:** read candidate drivers from the 9 cells, score by ETA (not straight-line distance), offer the top one.
612	- ⚠️ *What breaks:* downtown, those 9 cells hold ~18K drivers — scanning and scoring all of them per request, with ~100/s match requests funneling to that one cell-shard, makes the dense city the bottleneck.
613	- 🔁 *Forces upgrade:* the read-scan cost + concurrent matchers contending on the same hot cell.
614	
615	**#### Great: bounded top-K with concurrent-offer guarding**
616	- ↩️ *What the previous tier got wrong:* Good scanned all 18K and let concurrent matchers fight over the same drivers.
617	- 🗣️ *Plain words:* keep drivers pre-sorted by location so we grab just the nearest handful, and make sure two simultaneous requests don't both grab the same driver.
618	- **Approach:** maintain drivers in a **sorted geo structure** (Redis GEO sorted-set) so we read only the nearest ~20 candidates, not all 18K. Each offer is an **atomic CAS** flipping the driver to `offered`; a concurrent matcher that loses the CAS moves to the next candidate. Sub-shard the hot cell (from the firehose dive) so the scans parallelize.
619	- 🔢 *Decision math:* scanning 20 sorted candidates vs 18,000 = ~900x less work per match; at 100 matches/s on the hot cell that's the difference between feasible and melted.
620	- ✅ **Failure matrix:**
621	
622	| Scenario | What happens |
623	|---|---|
624	| Two riders, one driver | First CAS wins; second matcher skips to next candidate |
625	| All nearby drivers busy | Widen to next ring of cells, then queue rider with surge nudge |
626	| Driver goes offline mid-offer | Offer times out; sweeper re-matches |
627	
628	> 🎙️ **Script:** "In a dense city, naively scanning every nearby driver is thousands of rows per request. Instead I keep drivers in a sorted-by-distance structure so I only pull the nearest twenty. The other half is concurrency: two requests downtown might eye the same driver, so the offer is an atomic compare-and-set — first one wins, the loser just takes the next-best. Combined with sub-sharding the hot cell, the densest block stays fast."
629	
630	> 🧠 *If they ask "why ETA not distance?":* "Straight-line distance ignores a river or a one-way street — the closest car as the crow flies might be 15 minutes away. I score by routing ETA so the offer is actually the soonest pickup."
